summary: I was born in Chicago in 1914 and grew up on the outskirts of the city near Budlong Pickle Farm. Our neighborhood had gaslights that a man would light every evening, and the fire and police departments still used horse-drawn equipment. I lived in a house, not an apartment, and remember the mix of urban life with the nearby woods and farms.

Budlong Pickle Farm was right near where I lived in Chicago during my childhood. It was on the outskirts of the city, surrounded by trees and woods. This farm was a familiar landmark in my neighborhood and gave a rural feel to that part of Chicago at the time.

My father used to play tennis every Saturday with a neighbor named Kraft, who was starting out in the cheese business. He had a wagon and would go around selling cheese in the neighborhood, which was the beginning of what became Kraft Cheese Company. It was interesting to see how that small local business grew into a major company.

My full name is Emerson Seville Woelffer, named partly after my mother's father, Algernon Sidney Seville. Growing up in Chicago, I had a unique family background blending German and French-Swiss heritage. My experiences and memories from childhood shaped who I am.

My maternal grandfather, Algernon Sidney Seville, was in the music business with QRS Piano Rolls in Memphis, Tennessee. He worked on transcribing piano music onto player-piano rolls alongside W. C. Handy. He visited about once a year, always bringing me musical gifts, though I was more interested in painting when he gave me a large paintbox.

QRS Music Technologies was the company my grandfather, Algernon Sidney Seville, worked for in Memphis, Tennessee. They specialized in player-piano rolls, and my grandfather was involved in transcribing music onto these rolls. This connection gave me a glimpse into the early music technology world.

Memphis, Tennessee, is where my grandfather worked with QRS Piano Rolls, transcribing music for player-piano rolls. Though I lived in Chicago, I remember his visits from Memphis and the musical gifts he brought me. It was a place connected to my family's musical heritage.

W. C. Handy was a musician who worked with my grandfather, Algernon Sidney Seville, on transcribing music for player-piano rolls at QRS in Memphis. Their collaboration was part of the early music recording and reproduction efforts. Though I never met Handy personally, his name was part of my family's musical legacy.

De la Fontaine was my mother's maiden family name, and her two aunts came from Saint Croix, Switzerland. This French side of the family had a rich heritage, including a connection to the famous French fabulist. It was a proud part of my mother's lineage and our family's history.

Saint Croix, Switzerland, is where my mother's family, the Paillards, still live. It's a small town closely tied to my French-Swiss heritage through the De la Fontaine and Paillard families. The Paillards are known for making Bolex movie cameras and Hermes typewriters, linking my family to notable craftsmanship in that region.

My paternal grandfather worked in the meat-processing business alongside a man named Oscar Mayer. They made sausages together before Oscar Mayer started his own company, which became the famous Oscar Mayer brand. This connection shows how my family was involved in the early days of a major food company.

Paillard is the family name on my mother's side from Saint Croix, Switzerland. The Paillard family is well-known for making Bolex movie cameras and Hermes typewriters, and they originally started out making music boxes. This heritage reflects a tradition of fine craftsmanship in my family.

Bolex movie cameras were made by the Paillard family in Saint Croix, Switzerland, which is part of my mother's heritage. The Paillards have a history of producing quality products, starting with music boxes and later moving into cameras and typewriters. This connection is a point of pride in my family's background.

The Hermes typewriter was another product made by the Paillard family in Saint Croix, Switzerland, where my mother's family comes from. Along with Bolex cameras, the Hermes typewriter represents the family's legacy in manufacturing fine mechanical products. This heritage ties me to a tradition of innovation and quality.
